title: The Kubo-Thermalization Correspondence
authors: Songtao Huang, Xingyu Li, Jianyi Chen, Alan Tsidilkovski, Gabriel G. T. Assumpção, Pengfei Zhang, Hui Zhai, Nir Navon
abstract: Quantum thermalization describes how interacting quantum systems relax toward thermal equilibrium, a central problem in modern physics. Yet most experimental information on many-body systems comes from short-time transition spectroscopy, typically interpreted within Kubo's linear-response framework. These perspectives - long-time equilibration versus short-time response - seem fundamentally disconnected. Here we establish an exact link between them: the Kubo-Thermalization correspondence, which connects long-time thermalized magnetization under weak driving to short-time linear-response spectra for a spin coupled to a thermal bath. The correspondence holds even when the steady state differs substantially from the initial state and when each regime is individually difficult to describe theoretically. We experimentally confirm the correspondence using effective spin-1/2 impurities realized with ultracold fermions in two internal states coupled to a Fermi sea. Our results provide a rare exact statement about quantum thermalization and offer a novel route to infer thermalization dynamics from equilibrium response measurements in strongly interacting quantum systems, independent of microscopic details of the system-bath coupling.
summary: The paper establishes the Kubo-Thermalization correspondence, which exactly relates short-time linear-response spectra to long-time steady-state magnetization. This link is experimentally verified using ultracold 6Li atoms in the BCS-BEC crossover. The result provides a powerful tool to study thermalization in strongly interacting quantum systems using equilibrium measurements.
main_problem: Bridging the gap between short-time linear-response spectroscopy and long-time quantum thermalization in many-body systems.
main_result: Establishment of the Kubo-Thermalization correspondence, an exact functional link between the long-time steady-state magnetization and the short-time linear-response spectrum.
method: Theoretical derivation using analytic continuation and detailed balance, complemented by experimental verification using RF spectroscopy and magnetization measurements in ultracold Fermi gases.
model_or_system: A spin-1/2 impurity (encoded in two internal states of 6Li) coupled to a thermal bath of atoms in a third state, realized in a uniform optical box trap.
key_observables: Asymptotic magnetization (M_infinity), transition rate spectrum (R_down), spectral width (Gamma), and effective resonance frequency (Delta_0).
important_parameters: Inverse temperature (beta), s-wave scattering length (a_up_B), spin fraction (x), and spectral width (Gamma).
main_assumptions: The spin-bath interaction does not induce spin flips; the system is in a weakly driven regime; the spin-spin interactions are negligible due to high imbalance.
figures_summary: Illustrates the transition from narrow to broad spectra and the scaling of the deviation between spectral peak and magnetization zero-crossing as a function of interaction strength.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of disconnected dynamics, derives the Kubo-Thermalization correspondence theoretically, presents the experimental implementation using 6Li atoms, and demonstrates the correspondence across the BCS-BEC crossover.
why_interesting: It provides a universal, exact link between equilibrium response and thermalization dynamics, offering a new way to infer complex many-body thermalization from simple spectroscopic measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Experimental platform and the Kubo- Thermalization correspondence on the BCS side. (a) (Left) Dilute spin-1/2 particles (blue spheres) consisting of 6Li atoms interact with a bath composed of atoms prepared in a third state |B⟩(red spheres). The system is confined in a cylindrical optical box trap, in the presence of both a static tunable magnetic field B0 and a radio-frequency (rf) field. (Middle) In situ optical density image of the spin-1/2 particles. (Right) Schematic of the level structure, showing that the bath atoms are unaffected by the rf drive that con- nects |↓⟩and |↑⟩. FIG. 1. Kubo-Thermalization correspondence for a driven spin coupled to a thermal bath. (a) A spin-1/2 particle is immersed in a thermal bath at temperature T, and an external spin-flip term drives the quantum dynamics for a duration t with a weak Rabi frequency Ω0. (b) (Top) Dy- namical evolution of the magnetization M after initializing the spin in |↓⟩and turning on a weak spin-flip field. (Bottom left) The short-time transition spectroscopy R(∆), defined as the transition rate versus detuning, and ∆p is its peak posi- tion. (Bottom right) The long-time steady-state magnetiza- tion M∞(∆), characterized by its zero crossing ∆0 (defined as M∞(∆0) = 0). Our central result, Eq. (2), is a...

FIG. 3. Testing the Kubo-Thermalization correspondence across the BCS-BEC crossover. (a) R↓(∆) (blue circles, top), measured with ℏΩ0 ≈0.028EF and t = 4 ms (Ω0t ≈4) and the steady-state magnetization M∞(∆) (red circles, bottom), measured with ℏΩ0 ≈0.27EF and t = 20 ms (Ω0t ≈195). Both measurements are performed at 1/(kFa↑B) ≈0.5. The vertical red and blue bands indicate the values and uncertainties of ∆0 and ∆p, respectively, and the red solid curve corresponds to Eq. (1). (b) Difference ∆p −∆0 versus the full width at half maximum Γ. The black dashed line represents a power-law fit in log-log scale (see text). FIG. 4. The Kubo-Thermalization correspondence for the metastable (repulsive) branch. (a) Intensity map of R↓(∆) across different interaction strengths 1/(kFa↑B). (b) Top panel: Linear-response spectrum R↓(∆) (blue circles), measured with ℏΩ0 ≈0.003EF and t = 12 ms (Ω0t ≈1.4). Lower panel: steady-state magnetization M∞(∆) (red cir- cles) measured with ℏΩ0 ≈0.27EF and t = 200 ms (Ω0t ≈ 1950). Both measurements are performed at 1/(kFa↑B) ≈4.2, shown for detunings near the repulsive polaron energy. Ver- tical red and blue bands mark the values and uncertainties of ∆0 and ∆p, respectively.
